core_meaning: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> refers to a physical thing or object, but it also works as an abstract placeholder meaning 'something' or 'the thing that is...' In grammar, it often softens or explains a statement, adding nuance like 'that's just how things are' or 'it's natural that...'

sentences.jp: この<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>、どこで<ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>ったの？すごくかわいい！
sentences.translation: Where did you buy this thing? It's so cute!
sentences.notes: Basic use of <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> for a physical object, common in casual conversation among friends.

sentences.jp: <ruby>大切<rt>たいせつ</rt></ruby>な<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>は、<ruby>丁寧<rt>ていねい</rt></ruby>に<ruby>扱<rt>あつか</rt></ruby>わなければならない。
sentences.translation: Important things must be handled with care.
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> means 'things' or 'belongings' in a general, slightly abstract sense.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どもは<ruby>失敗<rt>しっぱい</rt></ruby>しながら<ruby>学<rt>まな</rt></ruby>ぶものだ。
sentences.translation: Children naturally learn through making mistakes.
sentences.notes: The grammar pattern ～ものだ expresses what is natural or expected — one of the most important grammatical uses of <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>.

sentences.jp: もっと<ruby>若<rt>わか</rt></ruby>いころは、よく<ruby>旅<rt>たび</rt></ruby>をしたものだ。
sentences.translation: When I was younger, I used to travel a lot.
sentences.notes: ～たものだ expresses nostalgia or reminiscing about past habits — a distinct grammatical use learners should recognize separately.

sentences.jp: いくら<ruby>高価<rt>こうか</rt></ruby>な<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>であっても、<ruby>心<rt>こころ</rt></ruby>のこもっていない<ruby>贈<rt>おく</rt></ruby>り<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>は<ruby>意味<rt>いみ</rt></ruby>がないと<ruby>私<rt>わたし</rt></ruby>は<ruby>思<rt>おも</rt></ruby>います。
sentences.translation: No matter how expensive an item may be, I believe a gift given without sincerity has no meaning.
sentences.notes: <ruby>贈<rt>おく</rt></ruby>り<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> (gift) shows how <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> compounds with other words; this sentence also shows a more formal written register.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> for abstract, uncountable concepts like love, time, or feelings — use <ruby>こと</ruby> instead (e.g. <ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きなことを<ruby>話<rt>はな</rt></ruby>す, not <ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きな<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>話<rt>はな</rt></ruby>す when talking about an activity). Don't confuse the grammatical ものだ with a literal object — context decides meaning. Also avoid using <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> when referring to people; use <ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby> or <ruby>者<rt>もの</rt></ruby> (written) for persons.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> refers to tangible objects or noun-like concepts with physical weight, while こと refers to abstract facts, events, or actions treated as concepts (e.g. <ruby>泳<rt>およ</rt></ruby>ぐことが<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きです 'I like swimming' vs <ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きな<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>はケーキです 'My favorite thing is cake'). A useful test: if you can point at it or hold it, <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> is likely right; if it's an idea or action, use こと. Both can appear in grammar patterns (ものだ vs ことだ), and those patterns have distinct, unrelated meanings.

frequency: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> is extremely common in both daily speech and written Japanese. The physical noun sense appears constantly in conversation, while the grammatical ものだ / たものだ patterns are frequent in both speech and writing. Learners will encounter it at every level.
anki_hint: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> = something you can hold OR a grammar tool: ～ものだ means 'that's how things naturally are,' and ～たものだ means 'I used to...'
